text: The actual pressure rises to fifteen, and the surgeon removes the Veress needle. The first optical trocar is introduced, directed towards the stomach, with an orogastric tube in place to deflate the stomach. The telescope is inserted, providing a view of the ovarian cyst. Using the baseball diamond concept, the surgeon marks the positions for additional ports, with the second port introduced at a distance of twenty-four centimeters.

After placing all the ports, the surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing extensive adhesions between the gangrenous ovarian cyst and the right abdominal wall and omentum. Blunt dissection is used to detach the cyst from the abdominal wall. Despite appearing on the right side, the cyst is confirmed to be left-sided. A puncture is made due to the large size of the cyst, which cannot be managed with a grasper alone.

The surgeon uses suction to aspirate the contents of the ovarian cyst. Following this, gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate recent adhesions involving the omentum, sigmoid colon, and surrounding tissues. The omentum is observed attempting to seal the gangrenous ovary, which is visible along with the torsion tube.

The surgeon observes the torted pedicle of the ovary adjacent to the uterus. The large ovarian cyst is visible, and the surgeon prepares to use an extracorporeal knot, specifically Mishra's knot, to tie the pedicle. A number one Vicryl suture is used, and the knot is tied with the assistance of a telescope and a grasper, feeding the suture four times.

The surgeon ties Mishra's knot around the root of the torsioned ovarian pedicle, using a knot pusher to secure it. The knot is tightened to prevent shearing effects. The suture is then cut, and the ovary is separated using a ligature, harmonic, or scissors.

The surgeon ensures at least six millimeters of tissue remain in front of the knot to form a secure dumbbell shape, preventing slippage. A grasper provides andromedial traction to maintain distance from the bowel. The ovary, identified as having a gangrenous cyst, is now separated and prepared for removal through the colpotomy wound.

The assistant pushes a sponge through the posterior fornix as the uterus is lifted. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the whitish-shiny vaginal fascia, located two centimeters below the arc of the utero-sacral ligament, ensuring the utero-sacral ligament remains undamaged.

The surgeon uses a claw forceps to hold the sponge that has been pushed through the vagina into the colpotomy wound. The bowel is adhered to the inflamed cyst, requiring blunt adhesiolysis to separate the immature adhesions. The claw forceps then hold the cyst, which is pulled into the vagina. Two cocker forceps are used vaginally to hold and bisect the cyst.

The surgeon uses a speculum to expose the cervix, while a Valsalam holds it in place. A cocker forceps is used to fix the thin part of the ovary, bisecting it and draining the cystic fluid with suction. The cyst is then maneuvered out using a zig-zag circular movement.

The surgeon carefully ensures that the rectum and other structures are not held as the cyst, now more than fifty percent out, is fully extracted along with the ovary. The cyst is then sent for histopathology. A thorough lavage is performed with at least three liters of saline, filling the abdominal cavity to wash out toxic fluid and reduce inflammation. The colpotomy opening is packed with a sponge and gloves, and the lavage is repeated multiple times before removing the vaginal pack to allow fluid drainage through the colpotomy wound.

The surgeon performs copious lavage of the abdominal cavity, shaking small bowel loops, sigmoid colon, and inflamed omentum to ensure thorough cleaning. The vaginal pack is removed to allow drainage of the lavage fluid through the colpotomy wound.

The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the abdominal cavity, injecting fluid to wash out toxins. The uterus covers the colpotomy wound, preventing herniation. The gallbladder, liver, and stomach are visible as fluid is injected using a roller multi with pressure for the second wash.

The surgeon performs copious lavage using a large jar to clear fluid from the abdominal cavity. The pack from the vagina is removed, and residual fluid from the interior cul-de-sac is suctioned out. The procedure utilizes small ports, with all being 5 mm except for the 10 mm telescope at Mishra's point.
